balanceSheet.title: Balance Sheet & Liquidity
balanceSheet.content:
Ask yourself: can this company survive a downturn?

What to look for:
• Current Ratio above 1.5: the company can comfortably pay short-term bills. Below 1.0 is a warning sign.
• Interest Coverage above 3x: debt payments are manageable. Below 1.5x means the company struggles to pay interest.
• Debt/EBITDA below 3x: debt is under control. Above 5x is a red flag.
• Total Equity growing over time: the company is building value for shareholders.
• Total Liabilities growing slower than Equity: the balance sheet is getting stronger.

💡 Compare Liabilities to Equity: if debt is growing much faster than equity, the company is taking on risk that could become dangerous in a recession.

epsChart.title: Earnings Per Share (EPS)
epsChart.content:
Ask yourself: is this company becoming more profitable over time?

What to look for:
• A rising EPS trend. This is one of the strongest signals of a quality business.
• EPS growing faster than revenue: the company is improving its efficiency and margins.
• EPS declining while revenue grows: margins are shrinking, which is a red flag.

How to use it in your analysis:
Compare the EPS growth rate to the stock's price growth. If EPS grows faster than the price, the stock is getting cheaper relative to earnings. That could be an opportunity.

💡 Sudden EPS jumps or drops in a single year may be due to one-time events, not real business changes. Always look at the multi-year trend.

revenueChart.title: Revenue & Net Income
revenueChart.content:
Ask yourself: is the business growing AND keeping more of what it earns?

What to look for:
• Both lines rising: healthy, sustainable growth.
• Revenue rising but income flat: costs are growing too fast. Worth investigating why.
• Income growing faster than revenue: improving efficiency, a great sign.
• Revenue declining: the business may be losing market share or facing disruption.

How to use it in your analysis:
The gap between revenue and net income represents total costs. If this gap narrows over time, the company is becoming more profitable per dollar of sales.

💡 Consistent growth over 3-5 years matters more than any single year. Look for steady compounding, not erratic spikes.

fcfChart.title: Free Cash Flow (FCF)
fcfChart.content:
Ask yourself: does this company generate real cash, or just accounting profits?

Free Cash Flow is the cash left after operations and investments. It's harder to manipulate than net income, making it a more reliable measure of financial health.

What to look for:
• Consistently positive bars: the business generates real cash.
• FCF growing over time: the company can reinvest, pay dividends, or buy back shares.
• FCF trend vs. Net Income: if Net Income is high but FCF is low, the company may rely on aggressive accounting or heavy capital spending.

💡 A company with strong FCF has options. One with weak FCF, no matter how high the reported profits, may struggle to reward shareholders.

incomeBreakdown.title: Income Breakdown
incomeBreakdown.content:
Ask yourself: where does every dollar of revenue actually go?

This chart breaks down the income structure so you can see how efficiently the company converts sales into profit.

What to look for:
• A larger Net Income slice (relative to Revenue) means better profitability.
• Cost of Revenue eating most of the bar means the company has thin margins and is vulnerable to cost increases.
• Operating Expenses growing faster than Revenue: efficiency is declining.

How to use it in your analysis:
Compare this breakdown across years. If the profit slice is growing, the business is scaling well. If costs are taking a bigger share, management needs to address it.

💡 Companies with high gross margins (small cost-of-revenue slice) typically have stronger competitive advantages, like pricing power or unique products.

fairValue.title: Fair Value Estimation
fairValue.content:
Ask yourself: am I paying a fair price, or am I overpaying for this stock?

Fair value estimates what the stock is truly worth, independent of market sentiment. We combine multiple methods (DCF, Graham, EPV, P/E-based) to reduce bias.

What to look for:
• Margin of Safety: positive means the stock trades below fair value (potential bargain). Negative means it's expensive.
• Fair Value Range: a narrow range means higher confidence in the estimate.
• Method agreement: when multiple methods converge on a similar value, the estimate is more reliable.

💡 Fair value is an estimate, not a guarantee. Use it as one input alongside fundamentals and market conditions. A stock trading below fair value isn't automatically a buy. Always investigate WHY it's cheap.

analystConsensus.title: Analyst Consensus & Earnings
analystConsensus.content:
Ask yourself: what does Wall Street think, and should I trust them?

Analyst ratings (Buy/Hold/Sell) aggregate professional opinions. Earnings surprises show how actual results compare to expectations.

What to look for:
• Consensus direction: useful as a sentiment gauge, but not a decision driver.
• Earnings surprises: consistent beats suggest conservative guidance and strong execution.
• Estimate trends: are analysts raising or lowering future estimates?

💡 Analysts tend to be overly optimistic and slow to downgrade. Use their consensus as one data point, never as the sole basis for your decision. The most valuable signal is the direction of estimate revisions, not the current rating.

valuationMetrics.title: Valuation Metrics
valuationMetrics.content:
Ask yourself: how does this stock's price compare to what the company actually delivers?

What to look for:
• P/E: how much you pay per dollar of profit. Compare to industry peers (tech ~25-30, utilities ~15-18). Low P/E + high growth = potential bargain.
• ROE above 15%: the company efficiently uses shareholder money.
• P/B below 1: could be undervalued, or could be a value trap. Investigate further.
• P/FCF: like P/E but uses actual cash flow. More reliable.
• Gross Margin: high margins signal pricing power and competitive advantage.
• ROIC above 10%: the company generates strong returns on ALL invested capital.

💡 The classic value investor's sweet spot: low P/E combined with high ROE and growing FCF. If you find all three, dig deeper. It could be a genuine opportunity.

ratiosRadar.title: Profitability Radar
ratiosRadar.content:
Ask yourself: what's the quick visual snapshot of this company's overall profile?

The radar chart plots key financial ratios on multiple axes, giving you an instant picture of the company's strengths and weaknesses.

What to look for:
• A wide, balanced shape: the company performs well across all dimensions.
• Collapsed or flat axes: these reveal specific weaknesses worth investigating.
• Changes over time: is the shape expanding (improving) or shrinking?

How to use it in your analysis:
Use the radar as a rapid screening tool. If most axes are extended, the company has a solid profile. If one axis is notably low, zoom into that specific metric to understand why.

💡 No company is perfect on every axis. Focus on whether weaknesses are temporary or structural.

fundamentalCriteria.title: Fundamental Analysis Criteria
fundamentalCriteria.content:
Ask yourself: does this company pass the quality checklist?

This section shows every criterion used to calculate the fundamental score, organized by result.

What to look for:
• Green (Passed): metrics where the company exceeds quality thresholds. More green = stronger fundamentals.
• Red (Failed): areas where the company falls short. Check the weight, because high-weight failures matter more.
• Gray (Unavailable): data not available. These are excluded from scoring and don't penalize the company.

How to interpret the score:
• Above 60: generally positive fundamentals.
• Above 75: strong fundamentals, worth investigating further.
• Below 40: significant concerns, proceed with caution.

💡 Don't just look at the total score. Read which specific criteria failed. A company can score 65 but hide a critical weakness (like dangerous debt levels) among many minor passes.

priceChart.title: Price Chart (Candlestick)
priceChart.content:
Ask yourself: what is the market telling me about this stock's momentum?

Each candle represents one trading period. Green = price went up, red = price went down. The thin lines (wicks) show the day's high and low.

What to look for:
• The overall trend: higher highs and higher lows = uptrend. Lower highs and lower lows = downtrend.
• Support levels: prices where the stock repeatedly bounces back up.
• Resistance levels: prices where the stock repeatedly pulls back down.

How to use it in your analysis:
Combine the price trend with your fundamental analysis. A fundamentally strong company in a price dip may be an opportunity. A weak company at all-time highs may be a risk.

💡 The price chart tells you what the market thinks. Your fundamental analysis tells you what the company is worth. When these diverge, opportunities arise.

technicalIndicators.title: Technical Indicators
technicalIndicators.content:
Ask yourself: is now a good time to buy, or should I wait for a better entry?

Technical indicators help you time your decisions, not make them. Use fundamentals to decide WHAT to buy, and technicals to decide WHEN.

What to look for:
• RSI below 30: potentially oversold (could be a good entry point). Above 70: potentially overbought.
• Price above SMA 200: the long-term trend is up. Below means long-term downtrend.
• Golden Cross (SMA 50 crosses above SMA 200): a bullish trend signal.
• MACD positive and rising: momentum is in your favor.

💡 Never rely on technicals alone to pick stocks. They're a timing tool, not a selection tool. A stock can be "oversold" and keep falling if fundamentals are deteriorating.

governance.title: Corporate Governance
governance.content:
Ask yourself: is the management team trustworthy with my money?

Even a great business can be ruined by poor management. Governance assesses whether leadership acts in shareholders' interest.

What to look for:
• Experienced leadership with a track record of value creation, not just tenure.
• Board independence: more independent directors means better oversight and fewer conflicts of interest.
• Compensation alignment: executive pay should be tied to shareholder returns, not guaranteed bonuses.
• Low risk scores: high audit, board, or compensation risk are warning signs.

💡 Poor governance is one of the strongest predictors of future problems. Even if every other metric looks great, excessive executive pay, insider selling, or frequent accounting restatements should make you think twice.

managementQuality.title: Management Quality
managementQuality.content:
Ask yourself: is this management team creating or destroying shareholder value?

This section evaluates management through three lenses:

• Piotroski F-Score (0-9): a checklist of 9 financial health signals. Scores of 7-9 indicate strong management. Below 4 is a red flag.
• Earnings Quality (OCF/Net Income): when operating cash flow exceeds net income (ratio > 1.0), earnings are backed by real cash. Below 0.5 suggests aggressive accounting.
• Share Dilution: negative values mean the company is buying back shares (rewarding shareholders). Positive values mean new shares are being issued (diluting your ownership).

Insider Activity shows whether executives are buying or selling their own stock. Consistent insider buying is one of the strongest bullish signals; insiders know their company best.

💡 A high Piotroski score combined with insider buying and share buybacks is the trifecta of shareholder-friendly management.

dividendAnalysis.title: Dividend Analysis
dividendAnalysis.content:
Ask yourself: does this company reward shareholders with reliable, growing dividends?

Dividends are a direct cash return to shareholders. For value investors, consistent and growing dividends signal financial strength and management discipline.

What to look for:
• Dividend Yield above 2%: reasonable income. Above 5% could signal risk (unsustainable payout).
• Payout Ratio below 60%: the company retains enough earnings to reinvest. Above 80% is risky.
• FCF Payout below 70%: dividends are backed by real cash, not just accounting profits.
• Growing dividends over time: shows management confidence in future earnings.
• Many consecutive years: 10+ years of uninterrupted dividends is a strong quality signal.

💡 A high yield alone isn't enough. Check if the payout ratio is sustainable and if free cash flow covers the dividend. Companies that cut dividends often see sharp price drops.
